title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CARB's Second Proposal of Amendments- Strengthening the LCFS Program
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(23rdâ€“ 29th September)
  • A total of 541,802 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 1.19x times rise week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades recorded a rise of 84.10% as 971,224 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price increased further this week to $63.79/MTâ€¯for Type 1â€¯transfers, recording a 10.31%Â rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 2.32% in prices.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price maintains above the $60 mark, rising 9.39% WoW; on 30th Sep, it was $65.50/MT.
  • Prices in the RFS market jumped this week, as D3 RIN prices rose 3.86% WoW, while D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices each increased by 15.52% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The LCFS market continues to demonstrate strong positive momentum, reflected in the rise of both average LCFS spot credit prices and Type 1 transfer prices this week, mirroring the trend observed in the previous week. The anticipated tightening of the market is a crucial factor contributing to the sustained rise in spot prices. In its recent proposed amendment, dated 1st October, CARB responds to the feedback received during the last public consultation period.
  • CARB has proposed amending the 20% crediting limitation to include sunflower oil used in the production of biobased diesel fuels. The revision specifies that this limitation applies to transactions involving transportation fuels used in California.
  • Additionally, CARB has proposed that the 20% limitation will not apply to any biomass-based diesel pathway certification applications submitted before the regulation's effective date up until January 1, 2028. This adjustment is intended to provide existing fuel producers with sufficient time to meet the 20% eligibility requirement and adapt their operations or feedstock supplies accordingly.
The proposed amendment also outlines several other proposed changes to the LCFS amendments. One notable proposal involves modifying the description of forest biomass waste in response to public feedback to align with forest biomass waste generated from activities that comply with local, state, and federal regulations. CARB also proposes reclassifying corn stover as a specified source feedstock, subject to chain-of-custody requirements, rather than sustainability certification requirements under LCFS regulations for special fuel pathway applications. Additionally, CARB proposes to permit book-and-claim accounting of biomethane for electricity generation used in electric vehicle (EV) charging, provided that the electricity is generated using fuel cell technology. This week saw a significant rise in RIN prices across all categories as there has been certain developments that reflect the biofuels industry's push for regulatory clarity, and prioritization of domestic production to support clean fuel producers within the US. Clean Fuels Alliance urged the Treasury to issue Â§45Z Clean Fuels Production Credit guidance by September to help biofuel producers transition smoothly before the credit takes effect in 2025. Growth Energy also filed petitions with the US Court of Appeals, challenging rulings on small refinery exemptions and an EPA emissions rule that overlooked biofuels' benefits in reducing emissions. Additionally, bipartisan lawmakers, led by Sens. Marshall and Brown, introduced the Farmer First Fuel Incentives Act to extend the 45Z credit for 10 years and prioritize U.S. feedstocks over foreign ones, ensuring American farmers benefit from biofuel tax incentives.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_588347" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 10.31% WoW rise from $57.83 to $63.79 last week. Additionally, all non-zero credit prices rose by 2.32% compared to the previous week. Type 1 trades saw 541,802 credits exchanged, recording a 1.19x times WoW rise this week, but the total transaction volume for All Non-Zero Trades had increased by 84.10% WoW this week, to reach 971,224 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_588348" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 9.39% WoW, reaching $66.05/MT. On 30th Sep, the average spot price was $65.50/MT, which significantly rose from $48.17/MT, the lowest point in August. The benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $66.98/MT as of 30th Sep, up from $57.67/MT as of 10thÂ Sep.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_588349" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 81 trades were reported in the market, with nine high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $83.00 for 7,500 credits of Type 2, and the lowest transfer by price last week was at $44.50 per credit of Type 2, reported at a volume of 5,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 75,000 credits in Type 1 were undertaken for a price of $64.00, and the smallest volume transfers of 21 credits, a Type 1 category, were undertaken for a price of $65.00/MT.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_588350" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] Trading activity in August experienced a significant downturn, with volumes dropping to 1.90 MMT from July's strong 5.21 MMT. Despite this decline, September saw a recovery, with volumes increasing by 43.39% to 2.71 MMT as of 29th September. Notably, Type 1 prices showed an upward trend in August, which persisted into September.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_588351" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 23rd â€“ 29th Sep, total VPT recorded a 6.83% rise (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose by 3.86% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 15.52% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Sens. Marshall, Brown, and Reps. Mann, Kaptur Lead Bipartisan Legislation Fighting For Farmers with Biofuel Tax Credit. U.S. Senator Roger Marshall, M.D. introduced the bicameral and bipartisan Farmer First Fuel Incentives Act requiring the Treasury Department to restrict the eligibility of the 45Z Tax Credit to renewable fuels made only from domestically sourced feedstocks and extending the tax credit to make it a full ten-year credit.Â  This bill is co-led with Senator Sherrod Brown (D-OH) with companion legislation introduced by Representatives Mann (R-KS-01) and Kaptur (D-OH-09) in the House of Representatives. Senator Pete Ricketts (R-NE), Amy Klobuchar (D-MN), Deb Fischer (R-NE), Tammy Baldwin (D-WI), and Tina Smith (D-MN) also cosponsored the legislation.
  • Proposed Rulemaking: Section 30C Alternative Fuel Vehicle Refueling Property Credit. The U.S. Department of Treasury and Internal Revenue Service on Sept. 18 released proposed regulations to provide guidance for the Section 30C Alternative Fuel Vehicle Refueling Property Credit.
  • DOE Announces $15 Million to Develop Innovative Systems for Clean Hydrogen Production. The U.S. Department of Energy's (DOE's) Office of Fossil Energy and Carbon ManagementÂ today announced up to $15 millionÂ in federal funding to make clean hydrogen a more available and affordable fuel for electricity generation, industrial decarbonization, and transportation. The funding opportunity will support research and development projects that will enable the low-cost production of clean hydrogen.
  • Clean Energy to build first private natural gas fueling station for Houston METRO. Clean Energy Fuels Corp. (NASDAQ: CLNE), the largest provider of the cleanest fuel for the transportation market, has announced a new agreement with the Metropolitan Transit Authority of Harris County (METRO), the regionâ€™s largest public transport provider, to build the agencyâ€™s first private compressed natural gas (CNG) fueling station. The new state-of-the-art facility will be the first of its kind and will exclusively serve up to 120 of METROâ€™s newest natural gas-powered transit buses operating busy routes throughout Houston and beyond.
